g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive lecture on Operating Systems - the essential software that makes computers work.
An Operating System is system software that acts as an intermediary between users and computer hardware, managing resources efficiently.
g.text: An OS is system software that manages computer hardware and software resources,
providing common services for programs. It acts as an intermediary between
users and hardware, making computers convenient and efficient to use.

g.data-narration: The primary goals of an OS are convenience - making computers easy to use, and efficiency - maximizing resource utilization.
An OS performs six major functions: Process Management, Memory Management, File System, I/O Management, Security, and Command Interpretation.
Process Management handles program execution, creating processes, scheduling CPU time, and managing inter-process communication and deadlocks.
g.text: A process is a program in execution. The OS handles:
• Process scheduling - deciding which process runs when

g.data-narration: Memory Management efficiently controls the computer's RAM, allocating and deallocating memory, tracking usage, and implementing virtual memory.
g.text: Efficiently manages the computer's main memory (RAM):
• Memory allocation and deallocation to processes
• Keep track of which memory locations are in use
• Implement virtual memory and memory protection

g.data-narration: File System Management organizes and stores data on disk, handling file operations, directories, permissions, and disk space management.
g.text: Organizes and stores data on storage devices:
• File creation, deletion, reading, and writing
• Directory structure management and file access control
• Disk space management and file backup

g.data-narration: I/O Device Management controls input/output devices through drivers, buffering, caching, and spooling for efficient operation.
g.text: • Device driver management - software to control hardware
• Buffering and caching for faster data access
• Spooling - queuing print jobs for sequential processing

g.data-narration: Security protects the system from attacks through authentication, access control, encryption, and malware protection mechanisms.
g.text: Defends system from threats and controls resource access:

g.data-narration: Command Interpretation provides the interface between users and the system through CLI text commands or GUI visual elements.
Operating systems are categorized into types: Batch, Time-Sharing, Distributed, Real-Time, Network, and Mobile - each for different purposes.
Time-sharing systems let multiple users share the CPU with time slices. Real-time systems have strict deadlines - hard for critical systems, soft for tolerable delays.
g.text: • CPU time divided into time slices
• Each user gets a turn rapidly

g.data-narration: The OS has a layered structure: Hardware at the base, then Kernel, System Calls, Services, Programs, and finally User Applications at the top.

g.text: OS Services (Process, Memory, File, I/O Mgmt)

g.data-narration: System calls are the programming interface between user programs and OS services, including process control, file management, and device operations.
In summary: OS acts as an intermediary, provides convenience and efficiency, manages processes, memory, files, and I/O, and comes in various types.

g.text: ✓ OS = Intermediate between users and hardware

g.data-narration: Common OS examples include Windows, Linux, macOS for computers, Android and iOS for mobile, and VxWorks for embedded real-time systems.
